title: Fair Value & Expected Value
subtitle: Centering quotes on the true mean
intro: Fair value is the price at which the contract is a fair bet — neither side has an edge. For a simple payout game, fair value is usually the expected value (EV): the probability-weighted average outcome.

Market makers center their quotes around fair value. If fair is 7 on a dice sum game, you might quote 6 at 8 with spread 2. If you center too high or low, informed flow will buy or sell against you until you adjust.

Interview puzzles love clean EV calculations: expected sum of two dice is 7; a fair coin pays 1 with probability 1/2 so EV is 0.5 per flip for a $1-on-heads contract; drawing one card from a shuffled deck, the expected rank depends on the payoff rule.
This lesson builds the arithmetic habit of computing fair value before you ever state a bid or ask.

Before you widen or skew, you need a number to anchor on. In dice-sum games fair is 7; in 'product of two fair dice' games fair is 12.25. Card games specify payoffs per rank — your job is to turn those rules into an expected value, then quote around it.

Brainstellar-style puzzles often hide fair value inside a story: 'You draw until you see a red face,' or 'Payoff equals the maximum of two rolls.' The market-making twist is that once you state fair, the room will test whether your bid and ask bracket it symmetrically unless inventory or information says otherwise.

questions.question: You market-make on the sum of two fair six-sided dice. Fair value of the sum is:

explanations.A: 6 is below the average — common guess but wrong.
explanations.B: Correct. E[sum] = 7 for two fair dice.
explanations.D: 3.5 is E[single die], not the sum.

explanations.C: Ten is the maximum payout, not the average.

Fair coin, $1 per head, $0 per tail. EV:

explanations.C: Full dollar only half the time.

questions.question: Fair value is 7. You want spread 2 centered on fair. Your quote should be:

explanations.C: Midpoint 6, spread 2 but not centered on 7.

remediation.question: Fair 50, spread 6, centered:
If your midpoint is below fair, informed buyers will:

explanations.A: Your ask is cheap vs fair — they buy.
explanations.B: Correct — ask below fair is attractive to buyers.
explanations.C: They'd sell to your bid if bid were above fair.

explanations.D: Hundred is max payout, not EV.

questions.question: Two fair coins flipped. Contract pays the product of the two results, where each coin shows 1 or 2 (not 0). Fair value of the product:

explanations.A: Too low — product can be 2 or 4 as well.
explanations.B: Two is possible but not the average.
explanations.D: Four is max product, not EV.

explanations.D: Three is maximum heads, not expected count.

questions.question: You make a market on the sum of two fair six-sided dice (each face 1–6, equally likely). What fair value should center your quote?

explanations.B: 6 is below the true mean — common guess but wrong for two dice.
explanations.C: 3.5 is the expectation of a single die, not the sum.
explanations.D: 12 is the maximum sum, not the average.

remediation.question: One fair die. Expected value:
Two dice sum: minimum and maximum outcomes are:

explanations.D: 1 is not achievable as a sum of two dice.

questions.question: Payoff equals the product of two independent fair dice rolls. What is fair value?

explanations.A: Correct. E[XY] = E[X]E[Y] = 3.5 × 3.5 = 12.25 for independent dice.
explanations.B: 7 is the expected sum, not product.
explanations.D: 36 is the maximum product (6×6), not the average.

remediation.question: E[X]=4, E[Y]=5, X and Y independent. E[XY]=

explanations.B: 9 would be E[X+Y] if means were 4 and 5... actually 4+5=9, not product.
explanations.D: 45 might confuse with sum of maxes.

questions.question: One card is drawn uniformly from a standard 52-card deck. Payoff = 10 if it is a heart, 0 otherwise. What fair value centers your quote?

explanations.B: 10 is the payoff conditional on hearts, not unconditional EV.
explanations.C: 5 might confuse with half the deck or wrong probability.
explanations.D: 0.25 is the probability, not the dollar EV.

questions.question: A coin contract pays $1 if heads; P(heads) = 0.6. You also collect a flat $2 rebate just for making a market, paid no matter what trades. Where should you CENTER your two-sided quote on the coin contract?

explanations.A: Correct. The contract's fair value is 0.6 × $1 = $0.60, so you center your market there. A flat rebate is a sunk subsidy — it boosts your P&L but does not move where the contract trades.
explanations.B: $2.60 adds the $2 rebate to the price. Quoting a $0.60 asset around $2.60 would get you instantly picked off — the rebate does not change fair value.
explanations.C: $1.00 assumes a fair coin; this coin is worth $0.60 in expectation.
explanations.D: $3.00 is far above fair value and would be picked off immediately.

remediation.question: P(H)=0.6, win $1 on H. Coin EV alone:
EV 0.6 plus certain $2. Total fair:

explanations.A: Correct — add certain cash to EV.
